expectations.text: Uses 'obsidian tag tag="#meeting"' (or similar correct syntax) to find notes by tag
expectations.evidence: Line 18 and 24: `obsidian tag tag="#meeting"` used to find notes by tag, both for preview and for the scripted loop.

expectations.text: Uses 'format=paths' to get file paths suitable for looping/scripting
expectations.evidence: Line 24: `obsidian tag tag="#meeting" format=paths` used explicitly for scripting. Also used in the loop on line 46 and in the full copy-paste sequence.

expectations.text: Uses 'obsidian move file=... to=...' to move files (not shell mv or cp)
expectations.evidence: Line 47: `obsidian move file="$note" to="meetings/"` inside the while loop. No shell `mv` or `cp` used anywhere.

expectations.text: Mentions or explains that 'obsidian move' auto-updates wikilinks (unlike shell mv)
expectations.evidence: Lines 6-7: "The `obsidian move` command is the only safe way to relocate files in an Obsidian vault. It automatically updates every wikilink across the vault that points to a moved file. Using the shell's `mv` command (or Finder/Explorer drag-and-drop) bypasses Obsidian's link updater and will leave all `[[wikilinks]]` broken."

expectations.text: Provides a loop or scripted pattern to move all matching files, not just a single example
expectations.evidence: Lines 46-49: `obsidian tag tag="#meeting" format=paths | while read note; do obsidian move file="$note" to="meetings/"; done` — a full shell loop over all matching files, not a single hardcoded path.

expectations.text: CLI parameter style is param=value, not --param
expectations.evidence: All CLI invocations consistently use param=value style: `tag="#meeting"`, `format=paths`, `file="$note"`, `to="meetings/"`. No --param flags used anywhere.

claims: obsidian move auto-creates the destination folder if it doesn't exist
obsidian move updates both bare [[Note Name]] and path-based [[path/Note Name]] wikilinks
~20 meeting notes are spread across 2-Areas/work/, 2-Areas/personal/, and vault root

suggestions: Consider adding a dry-run or echo-only loop variant as a safety step before the live move loop.
The guide could note whether `obsidian move` is idempotent (i.e., safe to re-run if a file is already in the destination).
eval_feedback.overall: Perfect score — all six assertions passed. The output is a well-structured, production-quality guide that correctly uses the Obsidian CLI, explains the wikilink safety rationale, and provides a complete scripted loop pattern. Execution was efficient (9 tool calls, no errors).
